text:
US military claims it has facilitated the transit of 380 million barrels of oil via the Strait of Hormuz since early May. 

(My math: If counting the whole of May, June and the first nine days of July, that would equal to ~5.4 million barrels a day)

MUST READ: “… But if we assume the Strait of Hormuz will remain at least partially open to ships and particularly oil tankers, the short-term impact of the war has not been cataclysmic….”

https://t.co/BjUmkkB6mT
U.S. oil consumption projections and outturns. The future often turns out differently than expected: https://t.co/jwXnUXmwV9

Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours.

US successfully escorted one group of tankers in through the Oman lane.

All of them had their AIS off, but there was one Indian cargo ship that had it on when it went through. The list can be seen below.

Following two days of US strikes, the US is back to escorting ships in. My guess is that it will try to escort a group out tonight, and Iran can either respond and create more escalation going into the weekend or it can lose leverage.

The Oman lane will be heavily contested in the coming days, which leaves either side with no choice but to escalate.

I have included the video from @MarineTraffic.
Explosions heard in city near to Strait of Hormuz - Mehr
Explosions in Iran's Konarak near Strait of Hormuz - Mehr #oott

The collapse in Chinese crude imports and refining runs—with little effect on domestic Chinese mobility—was by far the biggest unexpected swing that helped us avoid the worst of the Hormuz Crisis.

Check out my conversation with @DWnews about what we know and what we still don't. https://t.co/UrZQd9AIfs

re: US-Iran MOU and competing interpretations about what it means for control of Hormuz.

WSJ: 

"The root of the dispute is Paragraph 5, which says Iran will make arrangements to restore shipping through the strategic waterway and then work with Oman to determine how to administer it in the future. But it also includes an Iranian pledge to ensure safe passage and remove military obstacles such as mines.

Trump administration officials saw that clause as unlocking the strait, the main accomplishment of the president’s deal. Iranian hard-liners, however, have used it to push a maximalist interpretation that gives the Islamic Republic exclusive control over the waterway as a key source of leverage.

[...]

The parties agreed, seeing the language as necessary to close the deal but figuring they could assert their interpretations afterward, the mediators said."
Strategic oil reserve buying set to support crude demand through 2028 - Reuters
US Commerce Secretary Lutnick: Trump knows price of oil will stay low - Fox Business Interview
When your midlife crisis is to quit a stable 9-5 in order to track tankers around the clock without raising capital.
